topic_name: Type I and Type II Errors
hook: You already know that in hypothesis testing we set up a null hypothesis $H_0$ and decide whether to reject it based on a p-value and significance level $\alpha$. But here's the uncomfortable truth: even a perfectly executed test can lead you to the wrong conclusion — not because you made a calculation error, but because random sampling can fool you. What are the two distinct ways a hypothesis test can be wrong, and how do we measure the probability of each mistake?
concept: A hypothesis test makes a binary decision — reject $H_0$ or fail to reject $H_0$ — but the truth is also binary: $H_0$ is either actually true or actually false. A **Type I error** occurs when you reject a true $H_0$ (a false positive), with probability $P(\text{Type I}) = \alpha$. A **Type II error** occurs when you fail to reject a false $H_0$ (a false negative), with probability $P(\text{Type II}) = \beta$. The quantity $1 - \beta$ is called the **power** of the test.
anatomy: There are four structural components to understand. The **significance level** $\alpha$ is set by the researcher before the test and directly equals the probability of a Type I error. The **Type II error rate** $\beta$ depends on the true effect size, sample size, and $\alpha$. **Power** $= 1 - \beta$ is the probability of correctly rejecting a false $H_0$. The **decision table** organizes all four outcomes: correct rejection, correct non-rejection (true negative), Type I error (false positive), and Type II error (false negative) — each cell defined by the combination of the test's decision and reality.

worked_example.description_latex: We begin by identifying the hypotheses and the significance level. Setting $\alpha = 0.05$ means we are willing to tolerate a 5% chance of rejecting $H_0$ even when it is true — this 5% is exactly the probability of committing a Type I error, built directly into our decision rule.

worked_example.expression_latex: \begin{array}{c|cc} & H_0 \text{ True} & H_0 \text{ False} \\ \hline \text{Reject } H_0 & \text{Type I Error} & \text{Correct (Power)} \\ \text{Fail to Reject } H_0 & \text{Correct} & \text{Type II Error} \end{array}
worked_example.description_latex: This decision table maps every possible combination of reality and our conclusion. Notice that we can only know which cell we're actually in if we know the truth — in practice we never do, which is why both error types must be managed proactively before collecting data.

The probability of a Type I error is exactly $\alpha$ because we defined our rejection region to include exactly the most extreme 5% of the sampling distribution under $H_0$. If $H_0$ is true, random samples will land in that region 5% of the time purely by chance, causing a false rejection.

Unlike $\alpha$, the value of $\beta$ is not fixed by our choice of significance level alone — it also depends on how far the true parameter is from $H_0$ and on sample size. A large effect size or a large sample makes $\beta$ smaller, which increases power and makes the test better at catching real effects.

Here is the fundamental trade-off: if we decrease $\alpha$ to reduce the chance of a Type I error, our rejection region shrinks, which means it becomes harder to reject $H_0$ even when it is false — so $\beta$ increases. The only way to reduce both errors simultaneously is to increase the sample size $n$.

partial_example.description_latex: A pharmaceutical company tests whether a new drug has a higher side-effect rate than 10%. They set $\alpha = 0.01$ because falsely alarming regulators (Type I error) would be very costly. We need to identify what each type of error means in this real-world context.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Type I Error: Reject } H_0 \text{ when } p = 0.10 \text{ is actually true}
partial_example.description_latex: A Type I error here means concluding the drug's side-effect rate exceeds 10% when it actually does not — a false alarm that could lead to an unnecessary regulatory ban. This happens with probability $\alpha = 0.01$, which the company has deliberately kept small.

Now you describe the Type II error in context: what does it mean to fail to reject $H_0$ when the drug's true side-effect rate is actually higher than 10%, and who is harmed by this mistake? Write your answer and state what probability measures this error.

Because the company fixed $\alpha$ at a very strict 0.01 to protect against false alarms, $\beta$ will be relatively large unless they use a sufficiently large sample. This illustrates why sample size planning (power analysis) is done before the study begins — you must decide how much risk of each error type is acceptable.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A researcher sets $\alpha = 0.05$. What is the probability of a Type I error?
A test has $\beta = 0.20$. What is the power of the test, and what does it represent?
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Power} = 1 - \beta = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80; \text{ there is an 80\% chance of correctly rejecting a false } H_0

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A quality-control test checks whether a machine is producing defective parts at a rate above the acceptable level. Describe in one sentence what a Type II error would mean in this context, and state its probability symbol.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Type II error: failing to detect that the defect rate is too high when it actually is; probability} = \beta

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A researcher currently uses $\alpha = 0.05$ and obtains power $= 0.70$. She wants to achieve power $= 0.90$ without changing $\alpha$. What must she do, and how does this affect $\beta$?
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{She must increase sample size } n; \text{ this decreases } \beta \text{ from } 0.30 \text{ to } 0.10

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A court's standard is 'innocent until proven guilty.' Map this to hypothesis testing: what is $H_0$, what is a Type I error, and what is a Type II error? Which error does the legal system prioritize minimizing, and how is that reflected in the choice of $\alpha$?
practice_problems.answer_latex: H_0: \text{defendant is innocent}; \text{ Type I} = \text{convicting an innocent person}; \text{ Type II} = \text{acquitting a guilty person}; \text{ legal system minimizes Type I} \Rightarrow \text{very small } \alpha \text{ (high burden of proof)}

common_mistakes: Students often confuse which error goes with which situation by thinking 'Type I = one mistake, Type II = two mistakes.' The correct anchor is the null hypothesis: a Type I error is rejecting $H_0$ when it IS true (false positive), and a Type II error is failing to reject $H_0$ when it is FALSE (false negative). Memorize it by connecting Type I to 'crying wolf' — claiming an effect that isn't there.

Students frequently state that $P(\text{Type II Error}) = 1 - \alpha$, confusing the complement of $\alpha$ with $\beta$. In reality $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are not complementary; they are probabilities computed under different conditions — $\alpha$ under $H_0$ true, $\beta$ under $H_0$ false — and their sum can be anything between 0 and 2. The correct complement relationship is Power $= 1 - \beta$.

Students assume that choosing a smaller $\alpha$ always makes a test 'better.' In fact, decreasing $\alpha$ shrinks the rejection region and therefore increases $\beta$, making the test less sensitive to real effects. The fix is to recognize that both $\alpha$ and $\beta$ matter, and the only way to reduce both simultaneously is to increase the sample size $n$.
untested_variants: Calculating a specific numerical value of $\beta$ given a true population parameter, sample size, and $\alpha$ — requiring use of the sampling distribution under $H_a$ and z-scores.
Formal power analysis: using a power table or formula to determine the minimum sample size $n$ needed to achieve a target power (e.g., 0.80) for a given effect size and $\alpha$.
Comparing two tests with different $\alpha$ levels and the same $n$ and deciding which test is preferable based on the relative costs of Type I vs. Type II errors in a specific applied context.
Two-sided vs. one-sided tests and how the direction of $H_a$ changes the location of the rejection region and the resulting value of $\beta$ for the same true parameter value.
